agent_output: {"executive_summary":"Orchestrated saga pattern for a 4-step courier marketplace order flow (Order → Inventory → Payment → Courier). Each step has a compensating action; failures trigger reverse-order compensation of all completed steps. An Order Saga Orchestrator owns the state machine, providing clear failure semantics, auditability, and operational visibility without requiring distributed transactions.","architecture":{"components":[{"name":"Order Saga Orchestrator","responsibility":"Owns the saga state machine. Drives the forward flow step-by-step, detects failures, and triggers compensating actions in reverse order. Persists saga state for crash recovery.","boundaries":"Lives as a dedicated module within or alongside the Order Service. Does NOT contain business logic for inventory, payment, or courier — only sequencing and compensation logic."},{"name":"Order Service","responsibility":"Creates order records (PENDING), transitions order status through lifecycle (CONFIRMED, CANCELLED). Exposes createOrder and cancelOrder endpoints.","boundaries":"Owns the Order aggregate. Does not know about inventory levels, payment details, or courier availability."},{"name":"Inventory Service","responsibility":"Reserves and releases inventory for order line items. Exposes reserveItems and releaseItems endpoints.","boundaries":"Owns the Inventory/Stock aggregate. Reservation is a soft-lock with TTL — auto-releases if saga never completes."},{"name":"Payment Service","responsibility":"Charges and refunds customer payments. Exposes chargeCustomer and refundCustomer endpoints.","boundaries":"Owns payment transactions. Idempotent charge/refund via idempotency keys tied to saga ID."},{"name":"Courier Service","responsibility":"Assigns and unassigns couriers to orders. Exposes assignCourier and unassignCourier endpoints.","boundaries":"Owns courier availability and assignment. Assignment is tentative until saga completes."},{"name":"Saga State Store","responsibility":"Persists saga execution state: current step, completed steps, compensation log, and outcome. Enables crash recovery and idempotent retries.","boundaries":"Simple key-value or relational store. Could be a table in the Order Service database to avoid an additional infrastructure dependency."}],"interfaces":[{"from":"Order Saga Orchestrator","to":"Order Service","contract":"createOrder(orderId, items, customerId) → {orderId, status: PENDING} | cancelOrder(orderId) → {status: CANCELLED}","coupling_type":"synchronous command"},{"from":"Order Saga Orchestrator","to":"Inventory Service","contract":"reserveItems(sagaId, orderId, items[]) → {reservationId} | releaseItems(sagaId, reservationId) → {released: true}","coupling_type":"synchronous command"},{"from":"Order Saga Orchestrator","to":"Payment Service","contract":"chargeCustomer(sagaId, orderId, customerId, amount) → {paymentId} | refundCustomer(sagaId, paymentId) → {refunded: true}","coupling_type":"synchronous command"},{"from":"Order Saga Orchestrator","to":"Courier Service","contract":"assignCourier(sagaId, orderId, pickupLocation, deliveryLocation) → {courierId} | unassignCourier(sagaId, orderId, courierId) → {unassigned: true}","coupling_type":"synchronous command"}],"data_flow":"1. Client submits order request → Orchestrator creates saga record (state: STARTED). 2. Orchestrator calls Order Service → createOrder → saga state: ORDER_CREATED. 3. Orchestrator calls Inventory Service → reserveItems → saga state: ITEMS_RESERVED. 4. Orchestrator calls Payment Service → chargeCustomer → saga state: PAYMENT_CHARGED. 5. Orchestrator calls Courier Service → assignCourier → saga state: COURIER_ASSIGNED → saga state: COMPLETED. On failure at any step N, orchestrator walks backward from step N-1 to step 1, invoking each compensating action. Saga state transitions to COMPENSATING, then COMPENSATED (or COMPENSATION_FAILED if a compensation itself fails).","patterns_used":[{"pattern":"Orchestrated Saga","rationale":"Centralized orchestrator makes the multi-step flow explicit, debuggable, and auditable. Preferred over choreography here because: (a) 4 steps with strict ordering is complex enough that choreography becomes a hidden state machine spread across services, (b) compensation logic needs deterministic reverse ordering, (c) operational visibility — one place to query saga status."},{"pattern":"Compensating Transactions","rationale":"Each forward action has a semantic inverse. These are not database rollbacks — they are domain-level undo operations (cancel order, release reservation, issue refund, unassign courier)."},{"pattern":"Idempotent Operations","rationale":"Every command and compensation carries a sagaId used as an idempotency key. This allows safe retries after transient failures or orchestrator crashes without double-charging or double-releasing."},{"pattern":"State Machine","rationale":"Saga progress is modeled as an explicit state machine with well-defined transitions. This makes it impossible to skip steps, enables crash recovery by resuming from last persisted state, and simplifies testing."},{"pattern":"Reservation with TTL","rationale":"Inventory reservations have a time-to-live. If the saga stalls or the orchestrator dies before completing, reservations auto-expire rather than leaking inventory."}]},"domain_model":{"bounded_contexts":[{"name":"Order Management","classification":"core","aggregates":["Order (root: orderId, states: PENDING → CONFIRMED → CANCELLED, invariants: order must have ≥1 line item, status transitions are one-way except via saga compensation)","Saga (root: sagaId, states: STARTED → ORDER_CREATED → ITEMS_RESERVED → PAYMENT_CHARGED → COURIER_ASSIGNED → COMPLETED | COMPENSATING → COMPENSATED | COMPENSATION_FAILED)"]},{"name":"Inventory","classification":"supporting","aggregates":["Stock (root: skuId, invariant: available quantity ≥ 0 after reservation)","Reservation (root: reservationId, invariant: reservation expires after TTL, links to sagaId for idempotency)"]},{"name":"Payments","classification":"generic","aggregates":["PaymentTransaction (root: paymentId, states: PENDING → CHARGED → REFUNDED, invariant: refund ≤ original charge amount)"]},{"name":"Courier Dispatch","classification":"core","aggregates":["CourierAssignment (root: assignmentId, states: ASSIGNED → ACTIVE → UNASSIGNED, invariant: one active assignment per courier at a time)"]}],"context_map":[{"upstream":"Order Management","downstream":"Inventory","relationship":"Customer-Supplier — Order Management dictates what items need reservation; Inventory Service fulfills the request on its own terms."},{"upstream":"Order Management","downstream":"Payments","relationship":"Customer-Supplier — Order Management requests charges/refunds; Payment Service is a generic capability."},{"upstream":"Order Management","downstream":"Courier Dispatch","relationship":"Customer-Supplier — Order Management requests courier assignment; Courier Dispatch owns assignment logic and courier availability."}]},"trade_offs":[{"decision":"Orchestrated saga over choreography","gains":"Explicit control flow visible in one place. Easier to reason about compensation ordering. Simpler to add monitoring, alerting, and manual intervention. Easier to test — one state machine to verify.","sacrifices":"Orchestrator is a coordination single point — if it's down, no sagas progress. Tighter coupling to the orchestrator module. Slightly more centralized design.","alternatives_considered":["Choreography-based saga using domain events — each service listens for events and triggers next step. Rejected: compensation ordering becomes implicit and error-prone with 4 steps; debugging distributed event chains is significantly harder.","Two-phase commit (2PC) — rejected outright. Doesn't work across independent services with separate databases. Locks resources and doesn't scale."]},{"decision":"Synchronous command calls from orchestrator","gains":"Simpler implementation. Immediate success/failure feedback. No message broker dependency for the initial build.","sacrifices":"Temporal coupling — orchestrator blocks waiting for each service. If a service is slow, the entire saga is slow.","alternatives_considered":["Async messaging via a broker (e.g., RabbitMQ, Kafka) — better resilience and decoupling but adds infrastructure complexity. Recommended as an evolution path once traffic grows."]},{"decision":"Saga state stored in Order Service database","gains":"No additional infrastructure. Transactional consistency between order record and saga state. Simpler deployment.","sacrifices":"Ties saga persistence to Order Service's database. If Order Service DB is down, no sagas can start.","alternatives_considered":["Dedicated saga state store (separate DB or Redis) — more operationally independent but adds infrastructure and consistency concerns."]}],"flexibility_points":[{"area":"Communication mechanism (sync → async)","evolution_path":"Replace synchronous HTTP calls with message-based commands via a broker. The orchestrator's state machine logic doesn't change — only the transport layer. Interface contracts remain the same."},{"area":"Adding new saga steps","evolution_path":"The state machine is a linear chain. Adding a step (e.g., Notification Service after courier assignment) means adding one new state, one forward action, and one compensating action. No changes to existing services."},{"area":"Parallel step execution","evolution_path":"If future requirements allow it (e.g., inventory reservation and payment can happen in parallel), the orchestrator can fork the state machine into parallel branches and join before the next sequential step."},{"area":"Compensation retry policy","evolution_path":"Compensation actions can evolve from simple retry to exponential backoff to dead-letter-queue with manual intervention — all within the orchestrator, no service changes needed."}],"rigidity_points":[{"area":"Step ordering","reason":"The business rule requires strict sequential execution: Order → Inventory → Payment → Courier. This ordering is intentional and load-bearing — changing it requires re-analyzing compensation semantics."},{"area":"Idempotency keys","reason":"Every service must support idempotent operations keyed by sagaId. This is non-negotiable for correctness. Services that don't enforce idempotency will cause double-charges or double-releases on retry."},{"area":"Compensation semantics","reason":"Each compensating action must be the true semantic inverse of its forward action. These are domain-defined and cannot be auto-generated. Getting them wrong means data inconsistency."}],"risks":[{"risk":"Compensation action itself fails (e.g., refund fails after payment succeeded)","severity":"critical","mitigation":"Implement a compensation retry loop with exponential backoff. After N retries, transition saga to COMPENSATION_FAILED state and alert operations for manual intervention. Log full context for manual resolution."},{"risk":"Orchestrator crashes mid-saga","severity":"high","mitigation":"Saga state is persisted after each step transition. On restart, orchestrator queries for incomplete sagas and resumes from last persisted state. Idempotency keys ensure replayed commands are safe."},{"risk":"Inventory reservation leak — reservation made but saga never completes or compensates","severity":"high","mitigation":"Reservations have a TTL (e.g., 15 minutes). A background reaper in Inventory Service releases expired reservations. Saga timeout should be shorter than reservation TTL."},{"risk":"Partial observability — hard to debug stuck sagas","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Expose a saga status API that returns current state, step history with timestamps, and any error details. Build a dashboard for operations to view and manually intervene on stuck sagas."},{"risk":"Services return ambiguous responses (e.g., timeout — did the charge go through?)","severity":"high","mitigation":"On timeout or ambiguous failure, orchestrator must query the service for current state before deciding to compensate or proceed. Never assume failure on timeout — always verify."}],"implementation_guidance":{"recommended_approach":"Implement the saga as an explicit state machine with a saga record table. Each saga record has: sagaId, orderId, currentState, stepResults (JSON of completed step outputs like reservationId, paymentId, courierId), createdAt, updatedAt, errorDetails. The orchestrator is a function that takes a saga record, determines the next action based on currentState, executes it, persists the new state, and repeats. On failure, it switches to compensation mode and walks backward through stepResults to invoke compensating actions.","files_to_create_or_modify":["src/sagas/order-saga-orchestrator.ts — State machine and step execution logic","src/sagas/saga-state.ts — Saga state enum, transition rules, and type definitions","src/sagas/saga-repository.ts — Persistence for saga records","src/sagas/steps/create-order-step.ts — Forward: createOrder, Compensate: cancelOrder","src/sagas/steps/reserve-inventory-step.ts — Forward: reserveItems, Compensate: releaseItems","src/sagas/steps/charge-payment-step.ts — Forward: chargeCustomer, Compensate: refundCustomer","src/sagas/steps/assign-courier-step.ts — Forward: assignCourier, Compensate: unassignCourier","src/sagas/saga-step.interface.ts — Common interface: { execute(ctx), compensate(ctx) }","src/sagas/saga-recovery.ts — Startup recovery: find incomplete sagas and resume"],"patterns_to_follow":"Each saga step implements a common SagaStep interface with execute(context) and compensate(context) methods. The orchestrator iterates through an ordered array of steps. On success, it advances and persists. On failure, it reverses through completed steps calling compensate(). All calls include sagaId for idempotency. Step results are accumulated in the saga context so compensation has the IDs it needs (e.g., reservationId for releaseItems).","pitfalls_to_avoid":["Do NOT use database transactions across services — that's the whole point of the saga pattern.","Do NOT fire-and-forget compensations — you must verify each compensation succeeded before moving to the next.","Do NOT assume timeout means failure — always verify state with the target service before compensating.","Do NOT skip idempotency keys — without them, retries cause double side-effects.","Do NOT put business logic in the orchestrator — it should only know step ordering and state transitions, not HOW to reserve inventory or charge payments.","Do NOT use a single catch-all error handler — each step may fail differently and compensation logic depends on which step failed."]},"escalations":[],"confidence":{"level":92,"high_confidence_areas":["Saga pattern selection (orchestrated over choreography) is well-established for this type of sequential multi-service flow","Compensation ordering and semantics are straightforward given the linear step chain","State machine design and crash recovery patterns are battle-tested"],"low_confidence_areas":["Specific technology choices (message broker, database) are not prescribed — depends on existing infrastructure","Exact TTL values for inventory reservations depend on business SLAs not provided","Whether sync or async communication is right depends on traffic volume and latency requirements not specified"],"assumptions":["Each service has its own database (no shared DB)","Services are independently deployable","Latency requirements allow for sequential service calls in the initial implementation","The four services already exist or will be built to support the described interfaces"]}}
